# 📘 MODULE 1: FOUNDATIONS OF TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT

## 1.1 Introduction to Quality

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Quality** is the totality of characteristics of an entity (product, service, or process) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs. It is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfills requirements (as per ISO 9000:2015).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**
Imagine you walk into a restaurant and order a cup of coffee. The coffee arrives hot (not lukewarm), the cup is clean, the taste is consistent every time you visit, the server is polite, and the bill is exactly what was on the menu. That entire experience — from the raw bean to the smile — is **Quality**. It is not merely "no defects"; it is the *complete package* that delights the customer. In engineering, this translates to a car that runs smoothly, has zero breakdowns in the first year, looks elegant, is fuel-efficient, and the dealership treats you with respect. **TQM** is the philosophy of getting *all* of this right, *every time*, for *every customer*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**
> Quality is **not inspection** — inspection finds defects after they occur. Quality must be **built-in** through process control, prevention, and continuous improvement (Kaizen). This is the central paradigm shift of TQM.

## 1.2 Need for Quality

The need for quality arises from four universal pressures every modern organization faces:

1. **Competitive Pressure** — Global markets force firms to compete on quality, cost, and delivery. Inferior quality = loss of market share.
2. **Customer Pressure** — Today's customer is informed, demanding, and has alternatives. *Word-of-mouth* and digital reviews amplify the impact of poor quality.
3. **Cost Pressure** — Poor quality inflates costs through rework, scrap, warranty claims, returns, and litigation. **Prevention is cheaper than correction**.
4. **Social/Regulatory Pressure** — Society and governments demand safety, environmental protection, and ethical labor practices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Evaluation of Quality** historically evolved through **Four Eras**:
> 1. **Inspection Era (1900–1940)** — Detect defects after production.
> 2. **Quality Control Era (1940–1960)** — Use statistical methods (SPC) to control process.
> 3. **Quality Assurance Era (1960–1990)** — Build quality into the system; prevent defects.
> 4. **Total Quality Management Era (1990–Present)** — Organization-wide strategic commitment to customer satisfaction and continuous improvement.

## 1.3 Definitions of Quality (Classical & Modern)

| # | Authority | Definition | Emphasis |
|---|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Philip B. Crosby** | *"Quality is conformance to requirements (specifications)."* | Conformance |
| 2 | **W. Edwards Deming** | *"Quality is a predictable degree of uniformity and dependability, at low cost, suited to the market."* | Variability & System |
| 3 | **Joseph M. Juran** | *"Quality is fitness for use."* | Customer Suitability |
| 4 | **Walter A. Shewhart** | *"Quality results from a predictable degree of statistical control."* | Statistical |
| 5 | **Genichi Taguchi** | *"Quality is the loss a product causes to society after being shipped."* | Loss to Society |
| 6 | **ISO 9000:2015** | *"Degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfills requirements."* | Requirements |
| 7 | **Peter Drucker** | *"Quality in a product or service is not what the supplier puts in. It is what the customer gets out and is willing to pay for."* | Customer Value |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark answers, **always quote at least 3 different definitions** and synthesize them into a unified statement. Examiners award bonus marks for comparative analysis.

## 1.4 Dimensions of Product and Service Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **David Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Product Quality** (highly examinable — frequently asked for 7 marks):
> 1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics.
> 2. **Features** — Secondary, "bells and whistles" characteristics.
> 3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over time.
> 4. **Conformance** — Degree to which design/operating characteristics meet standards.
> 5. **Durability** — Useful life before replacement.
> 6. **Serviceability** — Speed, courtesy, and competence of repair.
> 7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound, taste).
> 8. **Perceived Quality** — Image and reputation.

**Dimensions of Service Quality** (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry — SERVQUAL Model):
1. **Tangibles** — Physical evidence, equipment, personnel appearance.
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably.
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers promptly.
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of staff; ability to inspire trust.
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention to customers.
6. *(Later extended)* **Access, Communication, Credibility, Security, Understanding the Customer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Product vs. Service Dimensions**
> Think of a **smartphone** (product) — it needs Performance (speed), Reliability (no crashes), Aesthetics (looks), and Durability (battery life). Now think of your **mobile network provider** (service) — it needs Reliability (network uptime), Responsiveness (call center), Assurance (competent staff), and Empathy (resolving your issue with care). Products are *tangible*; services are *experiences*.

# 📘 SECTION 2: TQM FRAMEWORK, GURU CONTRIBUTIONS, CUSTOMER FOCUS & COST OF QUALITY

## 2.1 Basic Concepts of TQM

**TQM = Total Quality Management** is a **management philosophy** that seeks to integrate all organizational functions (marketing, finance, design, engineering, production, customer service) to focus on meeting customer needs and organizational objectives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principles of TQM (Universal Pillars)**
> 1. **Customer Focus** — Customer is the ultimate judge of quality.
> 2. **Total Employee Involvement / Participation** — Everyone is responsible for quality.
> 3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Never-ending process of incremental improvement.
> 4. **Process-Centered Approach** — Focus on the process, not just the output.
> 5. **Integrated System** — All departments work as one interconnected system.
> 6. **Strategic & Systematic Approach** — Quality is a strategic weapon.
> 7. **Fact-Based Decision Making** — Decisions based on data, not intuition.
> 8. **Communication** — Effective two-way communication at all levels.
> 9. **Supplier Partnership** — Long-term relationships with quality suppliers.

## 2.2 TQM Framework

A generic TQM framework has **four pillars**:

| Pillar | Components | Outcome |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Pillar 1 — Foundation** | Ethics, Integrity, Trust, Training, Leadership, Recognition | Culture of quality |
| **Pillar 2 — Building Bricks** | Tools (7 QC Tools, SPC, FMEA, QFD, Benchmarking) + Techniques (Kaizen, Six Sigma, 5S) | Process capability |
| **Pillar 3 — Binding Mortar** | Communication, Teamwork, Participation | Organizational integration |
| **Pillar 4 — Roof** | Customer Satisfaction, Cost Reduction, Profitability, Competitive Advantage | Business excellence |

## 2.3 Contributions of Quality Gurus

### 🧓 W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993) — The Father of Statistical Quality Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's 14 Points for Management** *(Highly examinable — must be memorized for 14-mark questions)*

1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service.
2. Adopt the new philosophy (quality as survival).
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality (build quality in).
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone; minimize total cost.
5. Improve constantly every process — planning, production, service.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear, so everyone can work effectively.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

**Deming's PDCA / PDSA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle)**
- **Plan** → Identify the problem, plan a change.
- **Do** → Carry out the test/change on a small scale.
- **Check (Study)** → Examine the results.
- **Act** → If successful, standardize; if not, revise and loop back.

**Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (4 Components)**
1. Appreciation of a System
2. Knowledge of Variation (common cause vs. special cause)
3. Theory of Knowledge (epistemology)
4. Psychology of Change (human behavior)

### 🧓 Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008) — Father of Quality Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juran's Quality Trilogy (3 Processes)** — *The most important Juran contribution*

| Process | Japanese Name | Function |
|---------|--------------|----------|
| **Quality Planning** | Keikaku | Identify customers & their needs; develop product/process to meet those needs. |
| **Quality Control** | Kanri | Evaluate actual performance vs. goals; act on differences. |
| **Quality Improvement** | Kaizen | Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement. |

**Juran's 10 Steps for Quality Improvement**
1. Build awareness of need and opportunity.
2. Set goals for improvement.
3. Organize to reach goals.
4. Provide training.
5. Carry out projects to solve problems.
6. Report progress.
7. Give recognition.
8. Communicate results.
9. Keep score.
10. Maintain momentum by making annual improvement part of the systems and processes.

**Juran's Fitness for Use** — Quality = freedom from deficiencies (no rework, no scrap, no customer complaints).

### 🧓 Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001) — Father of Zero Defects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby's 14 Steps to Quality Improvement** *(Frequently asked)*

1. Make it clear that management is committed to quality.
2. Form quality improvement teams with representatives from each department.
3. Identify where current and potential quality problems exist.
4. Evaluate the cost of quality (CoQ) and explain its use.
5. Raise the quality awareness of all employees.
6. Take actions to correct problems identified.
7. Establish zero-defects committees.
8. Train supervisors to actively carry out their part of the quality improvement programme.
9. Hold a "Zero Defects Day" to let everyone know a new attitude has been adopted.
10. Encourage individuals to establish their own zero-defects goals.
11. Encourage employees to communicate to management the obstacles they face.
12. Recognize and appreciate those who participate.
13. Establish quality councils to communicate on a regular basis.
14. Do it all over again (continuous reinforcement).

**Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality**
1. **Quality = Conformance to requirements** (not elegance).
2. **Prevention, not inspection** (prevention is cheaper).
3. **Zero defects performance standard** (not "acceptable quality level").
4. **Measure quality by the price of non-conformance** (cost of doing things wrong).

**Crosby's Quality Vaccine** (Five-step disease-treatment analogy): Determination, Education, Implementation, Communication, Recognition.

## 2.4 Barriers to TQM Implementation

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall** — Examiners often ask: *"TQM failure is rarely due to tools; it is due to soft issues."*

| Barrier | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Lack of Top Management Commitment** | Without CEO-level sponsorship, TQM dies. |
| **Inadequate Training & Education** | Employees don't understand tools or philosophy. |
| **Resistance to Change** | Cultural inertia, fear of the unknown. |
| **Poor Communication** | Silos, lack of horizontal/vertical information flow. |
| **Short-term Focus** | Demanding immediate ROI; ignoring long-term gains. |
| **Inadequate Resources** | Insufficient budget, time, or manpower. |
| **Narrow Definition of Quality** | Limiting quality to products only, ignoring services. |
| **Lack of Customer Focus** | Internal processes prioritized over customer needs. |
| **Inappropriate Reward Systems** | Rewarding quantity over quality. |
| **Poor Integration with Strategy** | TQM as a "program" rather than a way of life. |

## 2.5 Quality Statements (Vision, Mission & Policy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Hierarchical Quality Documents**
> 1. **Quality Vision** — Long-term aspirational future state of quality.
> 2. **Quality Mission** — Fundamental purpose; what we do and for whom.
> 3. **Quality Policy** — Top management's documented commitment to quality, aligned with ISO 9001 Clause 5.2.
> **Quality Objectives** — Measurable targets derived from policy (e.g., *"reduce customer complaints by 20% in FY2025"*).

## 2.6 Customer Focus & Orientation

**Customer Focus** = Knowing the customer intimately — their current needs, future needs, and the processes that deliver value to them.
**Customer Orientation** = The *behavioral manifestation* of customer focus — every employee asks "How does my work affect the customer?"

**Levels of Customers:**
- **External Customers** — End users, retailers, distributors.
- **Internal Customers** — The next person in the process (e.g., the assembler is the customer of the parts supplier department).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moments of Truth** (Jan Carlzon, SAS Airlines) — Every time a customer interacts with your organization is a *moment of truth*. A typical customer has 30+ moments of truth per service. Each must be positive.

## 2.7 Customer Satisfaction

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition** — Customer satisfaction (CSAT) is the customer's perception of the degree to which the product or service has met their expectations.

**Key Models:**

**1. Expectation–Performance Model (Oliver, 1980)**
$$\text{Satisfaction} = \text{Perceived Performance} - \text{Expectations}$$

- If Performance > Expectations → **Delighted** customer
- If Performance = Expectations → **Satisfied** customer
- If Performance < Expectations → **Dissatisfied** customer

**2. Disconfirmation Paradigm**
- **Positive Disconfirmation** = Performance exceeds expectations.
- **Negative Disconfirmation** = Performance falls short of expectations.

**3. Kano Model (Three Categories of Quality)**
| Type | Characteristic | Customer Reaction |
|------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Must-Be (Basic)** | Expected, taken for granted | Dissatisfied if absent, never satisfied if present |
| **One-Dimensional (Performance)** | More is better | Satisfaction rises linearly |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | Unexpected, delightful | Huge delight if present, not dissatisfied if absent |

## 2.8 Customer Complaints

A **customer complaint** is any expression of dissatisfaction by a customer about a product, service, or the complaint-handling process itself. TQM organizations view complaints as **gifts** — free feedback that prevents future defects.

**Complaint Resolution Cycle (Recoverability):**
1. Acknowledge complaint within 24 hours.
2. Investigate root cause (use 5-Why analysis or Fishbone).
3. Communicate corrective action to customer.
4. Implement systemic fix.
5. Follow up to verify satisfaction.
6. Feed lessons learned into design/process improvement.

## 2.9 Customer Retention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Economics of Retention (Bain & Company 80/20 Rule):**
> - Acquiring a new customer costs **5–7× more** than retaining an existing one.
> - A 5% increase in customer retention can increase **profits by 25% to 95%**.
> - Loyal customers buy more, refer others, and tolerate occasional failures.

**Customer Retention Rate (CRR) Formula:**
$$\text{CRR} = \frac{(C_{\text{end}} - C_{\text{new}})}{C_{\text{start}}} \times 100\%$$

Where $C_{\text{end}}$ = customers at end, $C_{\text{new}}$ = new customers acquired, $C_{\text{start}}$ = customers at start.

**Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — simplified:**
$$\text{CLV} = \text{Average Purchase Value} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan} \times \text{Profit Margin}$$

## 2.10 Costs of Quality (CoQ) — The PAF Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joseph Juran's PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) Model** — *The standard framework for CoQ classification*

| Category | Cost Type | Examples |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Prevention Costs** | Costs to *prevent* defects | Quality planning, training, supplier capability assessment, design reviews, process capability studies |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Costs to *detect* defects | Inspection, testing, calibration, audits, inspection equipment |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, failure analysis, downtime |
| **External Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, legal costs, lost goodwill, complaint handling |

**Total Cost of Quality:**
$$\text{Total CoQ} = P + A + I + E$$

Where $P$ = Prevention, $A$ = Appraisal, $I$ = Internal Failure, $E$ = External Failure costs.

**Quality Cost Ratio (Industry Benchmark):**
- A mature TQM organization has: $P : A : (I+E) \approx 30 : 30 : 40$ (dominated by prevention and appraisal, low failure).
- A reactive organization has: $P : A : (I+E) \approx 5 : 25 : 70$ (high external failure).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark numerical questions, you will be given a cost table and asked to compute **Total CoQ, optimal cost mix, or to recommend action**. Always show unit conversion logic.

## 2.11 International Quality Standards

### ISO 9001:2015 — Quality Management System Standard

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO 9001:2015 (current 4th edition) — Clause Structure (10 Clauses)**
> - **Clauses 1–3** — Scope, Normative References, Terms & Definitions
> - **Clause 4** — Context of the Organization
> - **Clause 5** — Leadership
> - **Clause 6** — Planning
> - **Clause 7** — Support
> - **Clause 8** — Operation
> - **Clause 9** — Performance Evaluation
> - **Clause 10** — Improvement

**The 7 Quality Management Principles (ISO 9001:2015):**
1. Customer Focus
2. Leadership
3. Engagement of People
4. Process Approach
5. Improvement
6. Evidence-Based Decision Making
7. Relationship Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shift from ISO 9001:2008 to ISO 9001:2015** — The biggest change is the adoption of **High-Level Structure (HLS)** and **Risk-Based Thinking**. The 2008 version's "Preventive Action" clause was removed because prevention is now embedded throughout.

### ISO/TS 16949 → IATF 16949 (Automotive Sector)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Update** — ISO/TS 16949 was officially **superseded by IATF 16949:2016** in October 2016. KTU examiners still refer to "ISO/TS 16949" in legacy questions — students should mention IATF 16949 in answers and note the supersession.

**IATF 16949:2016** is a global automotive QMS standard developed by the **International Automotive Task Force (IATF)** in collaboration with ISO. It is based on **ISO 9001:2015** with **automotive-specific additions**.

**Key Requirements beyond ISO 9001:**
1. **Product Safety** — Mandatory program throughout the supply chain.
2. **Defect Prevention** — Use of APQP, PPAP, FMEA, MSA, SPC (the "Core Tools").
3. **Process Capability** — Demonstrated Cpk ≥ 1.33 for special characteristics.
4. **Customer-Specific Requirements (CSRs)** — Must be incorporated.
5. **Production Part Approval Process (PPAP)** — Required for new parts.
6. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Mandatory at design and process levels.
7. **Supplier Development and Monitoring** — Continuous.

**Significance in the Global Market**
- **ISO 9001** — Universal passport to global supply chains. Over **1.3 million certified organizations** in 170+ countries. Without it, most multinationals will not consider a supplier.
- **IATF 16949** — Mandatory for any supplier wanting to do business with major OEMs (Ford, GM, Toyota, VW, BMW, etc.). It is the *de facto* global automotive quality license.

| Standard | Industry | Key User Benefit |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| ISO 9001 | All sectors | Generic QMS framework, customer confidence |
| IATF 16949 | Automotive | Defect prevention, supply chain integration, OEM compliance |
| ISO 14001 | Environmental | Environmental management |
| ISO 45001 | Occupational Health & Safety | Worker safety |
| ISO 22000 | Food Safety | Food chain safety |
| ISO 27001 | Information Security | Data protection |
| AS9100 | Aerospace | Aerospace QMS |
| Six Sigma | Manufacturing / Service | Defect reduction (3.4 DPMO target) |

# 📘 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Total Cost of Quality

Starting from Juran's PAF classification, the **Total Cost of Quality (TCQ)** is defined as the sum of all four cost categories.

$$\text{TCQ} = C_P + C_A + C_I + C_E$$

**Step 1: Identify each cost from financial data**
- $C_P$ = Prevention cost = Quality planning + Training + Design review + Supplier evaluation
- $C_A$ = Appraisal cost = Incoming inspection + In-process testing + Final audit
- $C_I$ = Internal failure cost = Scrap + Rework + Re-inspection + Downtime cost
- $C_E$ = External failure cost = Warranty + Returns + Recall + Legal settlement + Lost goodwill

**Step 2: Sum them** — yields total cost of quality for a period.

**Step 3: Express as % of sales** (industry standard benchmarking):
$$\text{CoQ}\ \% = \frac{\text{TCQ}}{\text{Total Sales Revenue}} \times 100$$

**Why % of Sales?** Allows cross-industry and cross-firm comparison. Most world-class manufacturers operate at **2.5%–4% CoQ as % of sales**; reactive firms run at 15%–40%.

## 3.2 Derivation of Customer Retention Rate (CRR)

The standard CRR formula is:
$$\text{CRR} = \frac{(C_{\text{end}} - C_{\text{new}})}{C_{\text{start}}} \times 100\%$$

**Derivation logic:**
- $C_{\text{end}}$ = total customers at end of period (a mix of retained + new).
- $C_{\text{new}}$ = customers acquired *during* the period (subtract these because we want to measure retention of original base).
- $C_{\text{start}}$ = total customers at beginning of period (the "base" being tested for retention).
- The numerator $(C_{\text{end}} - C_{\text{new}})$ gives the number of original-base customers who stayed.
- Dividing by $C_{\text{start}}$ gives the fraction retained, expressed as %.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A mobile telecom company had 10,000 subscribers on 1-Jan. During the year, they lost 1,500 subscribers and gained 2,000 new ones. By 31-Dec, they had 10,500 subscribers.

- $C_{\text{start}} = 10{,}000$
- $C_{\text{new}} = 2{,}000$
- $C_{\text{end}} = 10{,}500$
- $\text{Retained} = C_{\text{end}} - C_{\text{new}} = 10{,}500 - 2{,}000 = 8{,}500$

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{8{,}500}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = 85\%$$

**Churn Rate** (inverse measure):
$$\text{Churn Rate} = 1 - \text{CRR} = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15 \text{ or } 15\%$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)

The simplified CLV formula:
$$\text{CLV} = A \times F \times T \times M$$

Where:
- $A$ = Average Purchase Value per transaction
- $F$ = Purchase Frequency (transactions per year)
- $T$ = Customer Lifespan (years)
- $M$ = Profit Margin (% as decimal)

**Worked Example:**
A coffee shop customer spends ₹250 per visit, visits 3 times a week (156 times/year), remains a customer for 5 years, and the shop's profit margin is 20%.

- $A = 250$ rupees
- $F = 156$ transactions/year
- $T = 5$ years
- $M = 0.20$

$$\text{CLV} = 250 \times 156 \times 5 \times 0.20$$

Step-by-step:
1. $250 \times 156 = 39{,}000$ rupees/year
2. $39{,}000 \times 5 = 195{,}000$ rupees over 5 years
3. $195{,}000 \times 0.20 = 39{,}000$ rupees

$$\boxed{\text{CLV} = \text{₹ } 39{,}000}$$

This tells management it is worth spending up to ₹39,000 to acquire and retain a similar customer.

## 3.4 Optimal Quality Cost Point (Economic Model)

The classical Feigenbaum-Juran economic model argues that the **Total CoQ curve is U-shaped** as a function of the level of investment in prevention.

$$\text{TCQ}(P) = C_P(P) + C_A(P) + C_I(P) + C_E(P)$$

As prevention investment $P$ increases:
- $C_P$ and $C_A$ **increase** (we spend more on prevention and inspection).
- $C_I$ and $C_E$ **decrease** (fewer defects escape).
- TCQ reaches a **minimum** at the optimal $P^*$ — the economic optimum.

$$\frac{d\,\text{TCQ}}{dP} = \frac{dC_P}{dP} + \frac{dC_A}{dP} + \frac{dC_I}{dP} + \frac{dC_E}{dP} = 0$$

This is the **first-order optimality condition**. The economic interpretation: at the optimum, the marginal increase in prevention+appraisal cost equals the marginal decrease in failure cost.

## 3.5 Worked Cost-of-Quality Numerical (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:**
A manufacturing firm collected the following data for one year (₹ in lakhs):

| Item | Amount (₹ lakhs) |
|------|------------------|
| Quality planning | 6 |
| Training in quality | 4 |
| Incoming inspection | 8 |
| In-process testing | 7 |
| Final inspection | 5 |
| Scrap and rework | 15 |
| Warranty claims | 18 |
| Product recalls | 12 |
| Customer complaint handling | 6 |
| Design review | 3 |

Total annual sales = ₹500 lakhs.

**Required:** (a) Classify each item into PAF categories. (b) Compute total CoQ. (c) Find CoQ as % of sales. (d) Comment on the cost structure.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Classification into PAF categories**

| Item | Amount | Category |
|------|--------|----------|
| Quality planning | 6 | Prevention |
| Training in quality | 4 | Prevention |
| Incoming inspection | 8 | Appraisal |
| In-process testing | 7 | Appraisal |
| Final inspection | 5 | Appraisal |
| Scrap and rework | 15 | Internal Failure |
| Warranty claims | 18 | External Failure |
| Product recalls | 12 | External Failure |
| Customer complaint handling | 6 | External Failure |
| Design review | 3 | Prevention |

**Step 2: Category-wise totals**
- $C_P = 6 + 4 + 3 = 13$ lakhs
- $C_A = 8 + 7 + 5 = 20$ lakhs
- $C_I = 15$ lakhs
- $C_E = 18 + 12 + 6 = 36$ lakhs

**Step 3: Total CoQ**
$$\text{TCQ} = 13 + 20 + 15 + 36 = 84 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 4: CoQ as % of Sales**
$$\text{CoQ}\ \% = \frac{84}{500} \times 100 = 16.8\%$$

**Step 5: Cost Mix**
- $P : A : I : E = 13 : 20 : 15 : 36$
- Prevention share = $13/84 = 15.5\%$
- External Failure share = $36/84 = 42.9\%$

**Comment:**
The firm has a **reactive** cost structure. The high share of External Failure (42.9%) — warranty, recalls, complaints — indicates customers are receiving defective products. CoQ at **16.8% of sales** is far above the world-class benchmark of 2.5–4%. The recommendation is to **increase Prevention investment** (especially design review and FMEA) to drive down internal and external failure costs. A target: double $C_P$ and reduce $C_E$ by 60% in two years.

# 📘 SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 TQM Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Foundation: Ethics, Trust, Leadership] --> A1[Culture of Quality]
    A1 --> B1[Brick 1: People Empowerment and Training]
    A1 --> B2[Brick 2: Quality Tools 7QC, SPC, FMEA, QFD]
    A1 --> B3[Brick 3: Continuous Improvement Kaizen, Six Sigma, PDCA]
    B1 --> C1[Mortar: Communication, Teamwork, Participation]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### ❓ Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Total Quality Management. List any four of its key principles.**

**Model Answer:**
**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members of the organization — from top management to front-line workers — to meet or exceed customer expectations.

**Four Key Principles of TQM:**
1. **Customer Focus** — The customer is the ultimate judge of quality; all decisions center on customer value.
2. **Total Employee Involvement** — Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor worker, is responsible for quality.
3. **Process-Centered Approach** — Quality is built into processes; we manage the process, the output manages itself.
4. **Continual Improvement (Kaizen)** — Improvement is a never-ending journey driven by PDCA cycles.

*(Alternative acceptable principles: Integrated System, Strategic Approach, Fact-Based Decision Making, Communication, Supplier Partnership.)*

---

### ❓ Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Focus** | Operational / product-oriented | System / process-oriented |
| **Nature** | Reactive — *detects* defects | Preventive — *prevents* defects |
| **Tools** | Inspection, testing, gauges, SPC | Audits, process design, FMEA, procedures |
| **Stage** | Post-production (often) | Upstream — built into design and process |
| **Responsibility** | QC department / inspectors | Everyone in the organization |
| **Objective** | Ensure product conforms to specs | Ensure the system produces conforming products |
| **Era** | Quality Control Era (1940–60) | Quality Assurance Era (1960–90) |

QA is the broader umbrella; QC is a subset of QA. QA is *system-driven*; QC is *product-driven*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question 3A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Repeated Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **Deming's 14 Points for Management** in detail. Discuss how they promote a TQM culture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Cost of Quality (CoQ)**. With a suitable example, classify the four categories of quality costs. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Answer — Part (a)

**Deming's 14 Points — Detailed Explanation**

W. Edwards Deming, the American statistician whose teachings transformed Japanese industry post-WWII, outlined 14 obligations of top management to achieve quality transformation. Each point is a *directive*, not a suggestion.

1. **Create constancy of purpose** — Innovate, plan, and invest for the long term, not short-term profits. Management must commit to staying in business by providing quality and service.
2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — Western management must awaken to the challenge and adopt a new economic philosophy of quality.
3. **Cease dependence on inspection** — Quality must be built in; inspection is too late, ineffective, and costly. Eliminate mass inspection as the substitute for sound manufacturing.
4. **End lowest-price purchasing** — Award business on the basis of *total cost*, not initial price. Multi-source sourcing kills quality.
5. **Improve constantly every process** — Continuous improvement of planning, production, and service reduces waste and improves quality.
6. **Institute training on the job** — Workers cannot perform well if they do not understand the system. Training is an investment, not a cost.
7. **Institute leadership** — Supervision should help people and machines do a better job. Leadership is the job of management; supervision is a different thing.
8. **Drive out fear** — Workers afraid to ask questions, suggest improvements, or admit mistakes are the most expensive ingredient in any organization.
9. **Break down barriers between departments** — Silos destroy quality. Sales, design, production, and finance must work as a team.
10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** — "Zero defects!" posters cannot solve systemic problems; they only irritate workers.
11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** — Quotas guarantee poor quality and high cost; they remove pride of workmanship.
12. **Remove barriers to pride of workmanship** — Annual rating systems, subjective evaluations, and misguided management destroy morale.
13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement** — Knowledge is the only lasting competitive advantage.
14. **Put everybody to work** — The transformation is everyone's job, led by management.

**How they promote a TQM culture:** They dismantle the old *command-and-control* paradigm and replace it with *system thinking*, *data-driven management*, *respect for people*, and *continual improvement* — the four pillars of modern TQM.

#### 📝 Model Answer — Part (b)

**Definition of Cost of Quality (CoQ):**
Cost of Quality is the total cost incurred in ensuring product/service conforms to requirements (prevention + appraisal) and the cost of failing to meet those requirements (failure costs). It is a managerial tool to measure and optimize the economic impact of quality.

**The Four Categories — Example of a Refrigerator Manufacturer:**

| Category | Cost Element | Example in Refrigerator Manufacturing |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Prevention Costs** | Quality planning, training, supplier assessment, design review | ₹20 lakh on FMEA of compressor design; ₹8 lakh on supplier development |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Inspection, testing, calibration | ₹15 lakh on in-line testing of thermostat calibration; ₹5 lakh on final inspection |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime | ₹25 lakh on reworking defective compressor welds; ₹10 lakh on scrapped sheet metal |
| **External Failure Costs** | Warranty, returns, recalls, legal, lost goodwill | ₹50 lakh on compressor replacement under warranty; ₹20 lakh on brand-recovery campaign |

**Total CoQ = 20 + 8 + 15 + 5 + 25 + 10 + 50 + 20 = ₹153 lakh**

**Managerial insight:** The high external failure cost (₹70 lakh) suggests defective product is reaching customers. A ₹15 lakh additional investment in prevention (FMEA + supplier development) typically reduces external failure by 50–60%, yielding a net saving of ~₹25 lakh — illustrating **Juran's economic principle of prevention**.

---

### ❓ Question 3B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Explain **Juran's Quality Trilogy** in detail. Compare it with **Deming's PDCA cycle**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **ISO 9001:2015**? Describe its **7 Quality Management Principles** and the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** of its clauses. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Answer — Part (a)

**Juran's Quality Trilogy**

Joseph Juran argued that quality management is comprised of three universal processes — *Quality Planning*, *Quality Control*, and *Quality Improvement* — collectively known as the Juran Trilogy. Every organization, regardless of industry, performs these three processes to one degree or another.

1. **Quality Planning (Keikaku):**
   - Identify customers (internal and external).
   - Determine customer needs (voice of customer).
   - Translate needs into product/service specifications.
   - Develop processes capable of meeting specifications.
   - Establish quality goals and metrics.
   *Output: A process that can meet customer needs under operating conditions.*

2. **Quality Control (Kanri):**
   - Evaluate actual operating performance.
   - Compare actual performance to quality goals.
   - Act on the difference (corrective action).
   *Output: Stability of operations; conformance to standards.*

3. **Quality Improvement (Kaizen):**
   - Establish infrastructure for improvement (council, teams, training).
   - Identify improvement projects (diagnostic journey).
   - Establish project teams, provide resources.
   - Apply quality tools to solve problems (remedial journey).
   - Hold gains, share lessons (institutionalize).
   *Output: Break-through performance and capability.*

**Comparison: Juran Trilogy vs. Deming PDCA**

| Aspect | Juran Trilogy | Deming PDCA |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **Scope** | Three broad management processes | One four-step scientific method |
| **Primary Use** | Strategic deployment of quality | Tactical problem solving |
| **Level** | Top management & middle management | Operational & project teams |
| **Improvement** | Explicit "Quality Improvement" process | Embedded in "Act" phase of repeated cycles |
| **Customer Focus** | Strong (Quality Planning starts with customer) | Implicit (in "Plan" phase) |
| **Complementary** | Often used together with PDCA within each process | Cycle can be applied within Juran's three processes |

In practice, **Deming's PDCA is the engine that powers Juran's Trilogy**. Each Juran process (plan, control, improve) uses PDCA cycles for execution.

#### 📝 Model Answer — Part (b)

**Definition of ISO 9001:2015**
ISO 9001:2015 is the international standard for Quality Management Systems (QMS) published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO). It specifies the requirements a QMS must meet to demonstrate the organization's ability to consistently provide products and services that meet customer and regulatory requirements. It is the world's most widely used QMS standard, with over **1.3 million certified organizations** in 170+ countries.

**The 7 Quality Management Principles:**

1. **Customer Focus** — The primary focus is to meet customer requirements and exceed expectations.
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and engage people in achieving quality objectives.
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered, and engaged people enhance the organization's capability.
4. **Process Approach** — Activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes forming a coherent system.
5. **Improvement** — Successful organizations have an ongoing focus on improvement.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Decisions based on analysis and evaluation of data are more likely to produce desired results.
7. **Relationship Management** — For sustained success, an organization manages relationships with interested parties (e.g., suppliers, partners).

**High-Level Structure (HLS) of ISO 9001:2015**

The 2015 revision adopted the **Annex SL High-Level Structure** to align with other ISO management system standards (ISO 14001, ISO 45001, etc.). It has **10 clauses**:

- **Clauses 1–3** — Scope, Normative References, Terms and Definitions
- **Clause 4** — Context of the Organization
- **Clause 5** — Leadership (replaces old Clause 5 Management Responsibility)
- **Clause 6** — Planning (includes risk-based thinking)
- **Clause 7** — Support (resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information)
- **Clause 8** — Operation (operational planning, design, production, release)
- **Clause 9** — Performance Evaluation (monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review)
- **Clause 10** — Improvement (nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement)

**Key Changes from ISO 9001:2008:**
- Introduction of **risk-based thinking** (replaces explicit "Preventive Action" clause).
- Use of **"documented information"** (replaces "documents" and "records").
- Adoption of **HLS** for alignment with other standards.
- Greater **leadership responsibility** in QMS.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — Students often **memorize only definitions** and lose 3–4 marks. To score full 14 marks, you must:
> 1. Give **at least 2 definitions from different authors** before stating your own.
> 2. Use a **diagram or table** (Gurus comparison, CoQ table, ISO clause structure).
> 3. End with a **conclusion linking back to TQM philosophy** ("Thus, ISO 9001 is the international framework that operationalizes TQM principles...").
> 4. For numerical CoQ questions, show **all 4 category totals separately** and provide a **brief managerial recommendation**.

1. **Quality** = *fitness for use* (Juran) + *conformance to requirements* (Crosby) + *predictable uniformity* (Deming) + *degree of fulfilling requirements* (ISO).
2. TQM is a **management philosophy**, not a tool.

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overviews

### 1. Benchmarking – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Benchmarking is the continuous, systematic process of measuring one's own products, services, and processes against the *best-in-class* performers within or outside the industry, in order to identify performance gaps and implement improvements that lead to superior performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Phrasing:** Benchmarking is **NOT** spying on competitors — it is a *legally ethical, documented, structured learning process* where the goal is to **adapt** (not copy) superior practices.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "School Topper" Method:**
Imagine a student named Riya who consistently scores 60/100 in mathematics. Instead of giving up, she finds the class topper (who scores 95), observes *how* they study (timing, materials, methods), and **adapts** those techniques to her routine. Within 6 months, Riya's score rises to 85. Riya didn't *copy* the topper's paper — she **learned from the best and improved herself**. That is exactly what Benchmarking does for organizations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of Benchmarking:** The term "Benchmark" originates from **surveyors** who chiseled reference marks (bench marks) into stone to measure elevations. The benchmarking concept in TQM was pioneered by **Rank Xerox (1979)** when it compared itself to Canon and Japanese rivals.

**Core Benchmarked Metrics (Industry-Standard):**
- **Cycle Time** (seconds)
- **Defect Rate (DPMO)** — Defects Per Million Opportunities
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** in **INR/USD**
- **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %**
- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)**
- **First Pass Yield (FPY) %**

---

### 2. Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition:**
FMEA is a **proactive, systematic, team-based analytical technique** used to identify potential failure modes of a product, process, or service, evaluate their severity, occurrence, and detectability, and prioritize corrective actions to prevent failures **before** they reach the customer.

**The "Pre-Flight Checklist" Analogy:**
Before every flight, pilots run through a pre-flight checklist — they don't wait for the engine to fail! They ask: *"What could fail? How bad would it be? How often? Can we detect it before takeoff?"* — and act on the answers. FMEA is exactly the same: a **pre-flight check for engineering and process design**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Origin:** FMEA was first formally developed by the **U.S. Military (MIL-STD-1629, 1949)** and later adapted for automotive (QS-9000), aerospace (AS9100), and medical devices (ISO 14971).

**The Risk Priority Number (RPN) Formula:**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- **S** = Severity (1–10): How serious is the failure to the customer?
- **O** = Occurrence (1–10): How frequently does the failure occur?
- **D** = Detection (1–10): How hard is it to detect the failure *before* it reaches the customer?

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on New AIAG-VDA (2019):** The traditional RPN has been **replaced** by an **Action Priority (AP)** matrix (H, M, L) in the updated FMEA standard, but **KTU exams (2024) still use the classic RPN formula**. We cover both for completeness.

---

### 3. Lean Manufacturing – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition:**
Lean Manufacturing is a systematic methodology (originating from the **Toyota Production System – TPS**) focused on the *elimination of waste (Muda)* in all forms, while delivering exactly what the customer needs, when they need it, in the quantity they need, without any waste.

**The "Refrigerator" Analogy:**
Open any household refrigerator. You'll find: 3-month-old leftovers, expired sauces, vegetables hidden behind milk cartons that rot unseen, multiple half-used bottles of the same condiment. **Lean** says: *Clear the fridge, only keep what you'll eat this week, arrange so the oldest is in front, and stop buying duplicates.* The 'waste' was hiding in plain sight. Lean identifies and removes that waste in factories.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD + 1) — Industry Standard:**
> 1. **T**ransportation (unnecessary movement of materials)
> 2. **I**nventory (excess stock / WIP)
> 3. **M**otion (unnecessary movement of people)
> 4. **W**aiting (idle time of machines/people)
> 5. **O**verproduction (making more than needed)
> 6. **O**ver-processing (doing more work than required)
> 7. **D**efects (rework, scrap, warranty)
> 8. **S**kills/Talent (underutilized human potential — added in modern Lean literature)

---

### 4. Fishbone Diagram (Ishikawa Diagram) – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition:**
The Fishbone Diagram, also known as the **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** or **Ishikawa Diagram**, is a structured brainstorming tool developed by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa (1968)** that visually categorizes the potential root causes of a problem (the "effect") into major cause categories, helping teams perform **root-cause analysis**.

**The "Detective's Board" Analogy:**
Think of a detective movie — a corkboard with a photo of the victim at the center, connected by red strings to suspects, weapons, and motives. Each thread is a *category of clues*. The Fishbone diagram is the quality detective's corkboard: the **"effect"** (the problem) is the head of the fish, and the **bones** are categories of possible causes. The team then "pins" specific causes onto each bone.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 6M Framework (Ishikawa's Original Categories):**
> - **Man (Workforce)** — skills, training, fatigue
> - **Machine (Equipment)** — condition, capability, maintenance
> - **Material (Input)** — quality, specification, supplier
> - **Method (Process)** — procedures, work instructions, sequence
> - **Measurement (Inspection)** — gauges, calibration, accuracy
> - **Mother Nature (Environment)** — temperature, humidity, dust, light
>
> **In Service/IT**, the 6M is replaced by the **4S Framework**: *Surroundings, Suppliers, Systems, Skills*.

---

### 5. Design of Experiments (DOE) – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition:**
Design of Experiments (DOE) is a **statistical methodology** (pioneered by **Sir Ronald A. Fisher, 1920s, Rothamsted Agricultural Station, UK**) in which deliberate, controlled changes are made to input variables (factors) of a process, in order to observe and quantify the corresponding changes in output variables (responses), thereby identifying the **optimal operating conditions** and the **dominant factors** affecting quality.

**The "Recipe Testing" Analogy:**
A chef wants the perfect chocolate cake. She suspects that **baking temperature (°C)**, **baking time (min)**, and **cocoa type (3 brands)** all matter. Instead of randomly trying recipes, she systematically bakes cakes:
- 180°C × 20 min × Brand A
- 180°C × 25 min × Brand A
- 200°C × 25 min × Brand A
- … and so on through every combination.
She **records** taste scores. From the data, she concludes: *"Temperature has the biggest effect; Brand A is best; 200°C × 22 min is optimal."* — This is **DOE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Core DOE Terms (must memorize for exams):**
> - **Factor (X):** The input variable we vary (e.g., temperature).
> - **Level:** The specific value a factor takes (e.g., 180°C, 200°C).
> - **Response (Y):** The output variable measured (e.g., cake taste score).
> - **Replicate:** A repeated run under the same factor levels (to estimate random error).
> - **Main Effect:** The average effect of one factor on the response, ignoring others.
> - **Interaction Effect:** The effect of one factor *depends* on the level of another factor.
> - **Experimental Unit:** The smallest entity to which a treatment is independently applied.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 1. Benchmarking — Theory, Types, and Process

#### 1.1 Types of Benchmarking (KTU High-Yield)

| **Type** | **What is Compared** | **Pros** | **Cons** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal** | Operations across branches/shifts of the *same* company | Easy data access, low cost | Limited fresh ideas | Comparing 5 factories of Tata Motors |
| **Competitive** | Direct competitor's product/process | Highly relevant to market | Data is hard to obtain (legally) | Maruti vs Hyundai |
| **Functional (Generic)** | Best-in-class *function* (not necessarily same industry) | Cross-industry innovation, novel ideas | Lessons may not transfer | Hospital benchmarks hotel check-in for patient admission |
| **Strategic (Process)** | Core business processes end-to-end | Strategic-level insights | Time-consuming | Apple vs Samsung R&D process |
| **Performance** | Performance metrics (KPIs) directly | Quantitative clarity | Doesn't reveal *how* they did it | DPMO comparison across plants |

#### 1.2 The 5-Phase Benchmarking Process (Robert C. Camp, KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robert C. Camp's 5-Phase Model (1989)** is the **most frequently tested** benchmarking process in KTU exams.

**Phase 1 — Plan:**
- Identify the process to benchmark (e.g., "Order Fulfillment").
- Define the metric (e.g., "Order delivery time").
- Identify the benchmarking partner.

**Phase 2 — Analyze:**
- Collect data on *own* process (As-Is).
- Collect data on partner's process.
- Compute the **performance gap** = $\Delta = P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}}$.

**Phase 3 — Integrate:**
- Communicate findings to leadership.
- Set *realistic* improvement targets (not unachievable ideals).

**Phase 4 — Action:**
- Develop an action plan (with timeline, budget, owner).
- Implement changes incrementally.

**Phase 5 — Maturity:**
- Make benchmarking a **continuous** habit.
- Recalibrate targets as you catch up.

**Mathematical Representation of the Performance Gap:**

$$\text{Performance Gap} = P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}}$$

**Percentage Improvement Required:**

$$\text{\% Improvement} = \frac{P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}}}{P_{\text{self}}} \times 100$$

#### 1.3 Reasons to Benchmark (KTU Top 7)

1. **Identify performance gaps** objectively.
2. **Learn best practices** without R&D cost.
3. **Set realistic performance targets** (vs arbitrary goals).
4. **Achieve competitive parity or leadership.**
5. **Foster a culture of continuous improvement.**
6. **Reduce cost of poor quality (COPQ).**
7. **Increase customer satisfaction and retention.**

#### 1.4 Applications

- **Manufacturing:** Cycle-time reduction, scrap reduction, OEE improvement.
- **Service Sector:** Hospital patient wait-time benchmarking, hotel check-in time.
- **IT Sector:** Help-desk resolution time, software defect density, Cloud SLA performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Service vs IT Benchmarking Examples:**
> - **Service:** Airlines benchmarking on-time performance (IndiGo vs SpiceJet).
> - **IT:** Infosys benchmarking its Agile sprint velocity against TCS/Cognizant.

### 2. FMEA — Deep Theory

#### 2.1 The Three Critical Ratings (Each 1–10)

| **Rating (S/O/D)** | **Score 1 (Best)** | **Score 5 (Moderate)** | **Score 10 (Worst)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Severity (S)** | No impact | Major loss of function | Safety hazard, injury |
| **Occurrence (O)** | 1 in 1,000,000 | 1 in 2,000 | ≥ 1 in 2 (almost always) |
| **Detection (D)** | Almost certain to detect | Moderate chance | Cannot detect / no control |

#### 2.2 RPN Thresholds (KTU Standard)

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

| **RPN Value** | **Action Level** |
|---|---|
| **< 100** | Acceptable — monitor |
| **100 – 200** | Reduce through controls |
| **> 200** | Mandatory corrective action |

#### 2.3 Types of FMEA

| **Type** | **Scope** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|
| **System FMEA** | Whole system-level | Concept phase, aerospace, defense |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Product design | Automotive (AIAG-VDA), mechanical products |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing process | Assembly lines, machining, casting |

#### 2.4 Stages of FMEA (8-Stage Standard, KTU 2024)

1. **Assemble a cross-functional team** (Design, Manufacturing, Quality, Service).
2. **Define the scope & boundaries** (product, process, customer use).
3. **Identify potential failure modes** for each function.
4. **Assess S, O, D scores** for each failure mode.
5. **Compute RPN** and rank failures.
6. **Prioritize** (RPN > threshold first).
7. **Recommend corrective actions** to lower RPN.
8. **Document & reassess** RPN after action.

### 3. Integration of Lean Manufacturing Principles with TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Question (14-Mark Favourite):** *"Compare TQM and Lean and discuss how they can be integrated."* — This appears **almost every university exam cycle.**

#### 3.1 The Five Lean Principles (Womack & Jones, 1996)

1. **Value** — Define value from the *customer's* perspective.
2. **Value Stream** — Map every step of the process; eliminate non-value-adding steps.
3. **Flow** — Make remaining steps flow without interruption.
4. **Pull** — Produce only when the customer pulls (downstream triggers upstream).
5. **Perfection** — Continuously improve toward perfection.

#### 3.2 TQM vs Lean — Similarities & Differences

| **Dimension** | **TQM** | **Lean** |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction, process quality | Waste elimination, flow efficiency |
| **Origin** | Deming, Juran, Crosby (1950s USA) | Toyota Production System (1950s Japan) |
| **Approach** | Broad — covers entire organization | Narrow — focuses on process flow |
| **Key Tools** | SPC, FMEA, QFD, Pareto | VSM, 5S, Kanban, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke |
| **Metric** | Defects per Million (DPMO), σ-level | Lead time, Takt time, OEE |
| **Mindset** | "Do it right the first time" | "Eliminate what doesn't add value" |

#### 3.3 Integration — How Lean & TQM Complement Each Other

- **TQM = "What" to improve** (Customer-driven quality goals)
- **Lean = "How" to improve** (Waste-eliminating tools)
- **Combined = Lean Six Sigma (LSS):** Uses **Lean's speed** + **Six Sigma's data-driven DMAIC rigor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Synergy Framework:**
> - TQM provides the **philosophy & culture** (Kaizen mindset, customer obsession).
> - Lean provides the **operational tools** (VSM, 5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke).
> - The integration is sometimes called **"Lean TQM"** or **"Total Lean Management."**

### 5. Design of Experiments (DOE) — Theoretical Foundation

#### 5.1 Why DOE? (The Power of Structured Testing)

Without DOE, varying **k factors at 2 levels** one-at-a-time (OFAT) requires $2k$ runs and **misses interactions**.

DOE's full-factorial design requires $2^k$ runs but **captures all main effects AND interactions**.

| **# of Factors (k)** | **OFAT Runs (One-Factor-At-A-Time)** | **$2^k$ Full-Factorial Runs** |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 4 | 4 (same) |
| 3 | 6 | 8 |
| 4 | 8 | 16 |
| 5 | 10 | 32 |

#### 5.2 Types of DOE (KTU High-Yield)

| **Type** | **Runs Required** | **Use** | **Catches Interactions?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Factorial $2^k$** | $2^k$ | All main + all interactions | Yes, all |
| **Fractional Factorial $2^{k-p}$** | $2^{k-p}$ | Screening, large k | Only some (assumed few active) |
| **Taguchi (Orthogonal Arrays, e.g., L4, L9, L27)** | Small (L9 = 9 runs for 4 factors at 3 levels) | Robust design, industrial | Pre-defined resolution |
| **Response Surface (RSM, e.g., CCD, Box-Behnken)** | 15–50+ | Optimization after screening | Yes (quadratic) |
| **One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT)** | $2k$ | Quick checks | **No** |

#### 5.3 The $2^k$ Factorial Design — Key Equations

For a **2-factor, 2-level** design (a $2^2$ design) with factors $A$ and $B$ at levels $(-1, +1)$:

The linear model is:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 A + \beta_2 B + \beta_{12} AB + \varepsilon$$

Where:
- $\beta_0$ = grand mean
- $\beta_1$ = main effect of A
- $\beta_2$ = main effect of B
- $\beta_{12}$ = interaction effect of A×B
- $\varepsilon$ = random error

**Main Effect Calculation (for factor A, signed in coded units):**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\text{Sum of }Y \text{ at }A=+1 - \text{Sum of }Y \text{ at }A=-1}{2^{k-1} \cdot n}$$

For a **single replicate** ($n=1$) of $2^2$ design, with runs $(A,B) = (-,-), (+,-), (-,+), (+,+)$ giving responses $y_1, y_2, y_3, y_4$:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{-y_1 + y_2 - y_3 + y_4}{4}$$

$$\beta_2 = \frac{-y_1 - y_2 + y_3 + y_4}{4}$$

$$\beta_{12} = \frac{+y_1 - y_2 - y_3 + y_4}{4}$$

#### 5.4 Steps in DOE (KTU 5-Step Process)

1. **State the problem** and identify the response $Y$.
2. **Identify factors and levels** (use fishbone + Pareto to narrow down).
3. **Select the design** (full / fractional / Taguchi).
4. **Conduct the experiment** (randomize run order to avoid bias).
5. **Analyze** (ANOVA, main effects plot, interaction plot) and **confirm** with confirmation runs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students confuse "factors" with "levels." Quick memory aid: **"Factor is the ingredient, Level is the amount of that ingredient."**

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 1. Worked Example: Benchmarking Performance Gap Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style 3-mark / short):**
A company benchmarks its order delivery time against a partner. The company delivers in **120 hours**, and the partner delivers in **72 hours**. Calculate:
(i) The performance gap in hours.
(ii) The percentage improvement required.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
- $P_{\text{self}} = 120$ hours
- $P_{\text{partner}} = 72$ hours

**Step 2 — Apply the performance gap formula.**

$$\text{Performance Gap} = P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}} = 72 - 120 = -48 \text{ hours}$$

The negative sign indicates the company is **48 hours slower** than the partner.

**Step 3 — Apply the percentage improvement formula.**

$$\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}}}{P_{\text{self}}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{72 - 120}{120} \times 100 = \frac{-48}{120} \times 100 = -40\%$$

**Result:** The company must improve its order delivery time by **40%** to match the benchmarking partner.

---

### 2. Worked Example: FMEA RPN Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU 7-mark style):**
In a CNC machining process, consider the failure mode **"Tool wear causes dimensional deviation."** The quality team has assessed:
- Severity $S = 8$ (major dimensional non-conformance)
- Occurrence $O = 5$ (occurs 1 in 2000 cycles)
- Detection $D = 4$ (caught by SPC chart with some delay)

Compute the RPN and state the recommended action.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the RPN formula.**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product.**

$$RPN = 160$$

**Step 4 — Compare to the threshold.**
- $RPN = 160$ lies in the range **100 – 200** → **"Reduce through controls"** action level.

**Step 5 — Recommend corrective action.**
- Introduce **automatic tool-offset compensation** (reduces O).
- Add **in-process probing** (reduces D).
- Add **tool-life management software** (reduces O further).
- After action, RPN must be **recalculated** and should ideally drop below 100.

---

### 3. Worked Example: $2^2$ Factorial DOE — Effect Estimation

**Problem Statement (KTU 14-mark style):**
A process engineer studies the effect of two factors on surface roughness ($R_a$ in $\mu m$):
- Factor **A** = Cutting Speed (low = $-1$: 100 m/min, high = $+1$: 200 m/min)
- Factor **B** = Feed Rate (low = $-1$: 0.1 mm/rev, high = $+1$: 0.2 mm/rev)

The four runs (in coded units) yielded the following $R_a$ values:

| **Run** | **A** | **B** | **$R_a$ ($\mu m$)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-1$ | $-1$ | 1.2 |
| 2 | $+1$ | $-1$ | 0.8 |
| 3 | $-1$ | $+1$ | 1.6 |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | 0.4 |

**Required:** Calculate the main effect of A, the main effect of B, and the interaction effect AB. State which factor is dominant and whether the interaction is significant.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the effect formulas for a $2^2$ design with single replicate.**

The $2^2$ design has a $2 \times 2$ sign matrix where each column's sign for a run is given by the standard Yates pattern:

| **Run** | **I** | **A** | **B** | **AB** | **$Y$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | $y_1 = 1.2$ |
| 2 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | $y_2 = 0.8$ |
| 3 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | $-1$ | $y_3 = 1.6$ |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | $y_4 = 0.4$ |

**Step 2 — Calculate the grand mean $\beta_0$ (intercept).**

$$\beta_0 = \frac{y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + y_4}{4} = \frac{1.2 + 0.8 + 1.6 + 0.4}{4} = \frac{4.0}{4} = 1.0 \text{ } \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the main effect of A (Cutting Speed).**

Multiply each $Y$ by the sign in column A and sum, then divide by 4:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{(-1)(1.2) + (+1)(0.8) + (-1)(1.6) + (+1)(0.4)}{4}$$

$$\beta_1 = \frac{-1.2 + 0.8 - 1.6 + 0.4}{4} = \frac{-1.6}{4} = -0.4 \text{ } \mu m$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the main effect of B (Feed Rate).**

$$\beta_2 = \frac{(-1)(1.2) + (-1)(0.8) + (+1)(1.6) + (+1)(0.4)}{4}$$

$$\beta_2 = \frac{-1.2 - 0.8 + 1.6 + 0.4}{4} = \frac{0.0}{4} = 0.0 \text{ } \mu m$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the interaction effect AB.**

$$\beta_{12} = \frac{(+1)(1.2) + (-1)(0.8) + (-1)(1.6) + (+1)(0.4)}{4}$$

$$\beta_{12} = \frac{1.2 - 0.8 - 1.6 + 0.4}{4} = \frac{-0.8}{4} = -0.2 \text{ } \mu m$$

**Step 6 — Write the fitted model.**

$$Y = 1.0 - 0.4 A + 0.0 B - 0.2 AB + \varepsilon$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the results.**

| **Term** | **Magnitude** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta_0 = 1.0$ | — | Average roughness is 1.0 μm |
| $\beta_1 = -0.4$ | **Largest** | Cutting Speed has the **strongest** effect. Moving from low to high speed **reduces roughness by 0.4 μm** |
| $\beta_2 = 0.0$ | Zero | Feed Rate has **no main effect** in this data |
| $\beta_{12} = -0.2$ | Moderate | A×B interaction exists: the effect of speed **depends** on feed rate (worth investigating further) |

**Step 8 — Practical conclusion.**
The optimal setting is **High Speed ($+1$) and High Feed ($+1$)**, giving the lowest observed $R_a = 0.4$ μm. To confirm statistical significance, an **ANOVA table with replication** would be required (out of KTU scope for the 14-mark problem, but mention it for full marks).

### 4. Worked Example: Fishbone Diagram — 5-Why Analysis

**Problem Statement (KTU 7-mark style):**
A factory observes an **increase in weld defects** (effect). Using the **Fishbone (6M) framework** and **5-Why analysis**, drill down to the root cause.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct the 6M framework (table form since diagram drawing is verbal here):**

| **6M Category** | **Brainstormed Cause** | **5-Why Drilling** |
|---|---|---|
| **Man** | Welder skipped training | Why? → Training schedule conflict. Why? → New welder hired last week. **Root cause: No onboarding process.** |
| **Machine** | Welding torch voltage fluctuating | Why? → Power supply unstable. Why? → No UPS on shop floor. **Root cause: Missing power backup.** |
| **Material** | Electrode coating varies batch-to-batch | Why? → Multiple suppliers. Why? → No supplier audit. **Root cause: No Approved Vendor List enforcement.** |
| **Method** | No standard weld parameter sheet at station | Why? → SOP outdated. Why? → No document control. **Root cause: Missing document control system.** |
| **Measurement** | Visual inspection only, no NDT | Why? → NDT equipment unavailable. Why? → Budget not allocated. **Root cause: Inspection under-investment.** |
| **Mother Nature** | Workshop humidity > 80% in monsoon | Why? → No climate control. Why? → Plant not designed for humidity. **Root cause: Layout / HVAC gap.** |

**Step 2 — Rank the root causes by impact (Pareto prioritization).**

**Step 3 — Recommend top 3 corrective actions:**
1. Implement document control + update WPS/PQR (Method).
2. Add NDT (UT / RT) at sampling stations (Measurement).
3. Enforce single-source qualified electrodes with COC (Material).

**Step 4 — Verify** by re-running the FMEA on the updated process.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Benchmarking. List any three reasons why an organization should adopt benchmarking.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Benchmarking is the continuous, systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against those of best-in-class performers, in order to identify performance gaps and implement improvements.

**Three Reasons:**
1. **Identify performance gaps** objectively rather than relying on intuition.
2. **Set realistic, data-driven improvement targets** based on proven performance levels.
3. **Learn industry best practices** without incurring the full R&D cost of original innovation.
4. *(Optional)* Foster a culture of continuous improvement and increase customer satisfaction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? Write the formula and explain the three components.**

**Model Answer:**

**RPN Definition:** RPN is a numerical score used in FMEA to rank the relative risk of each identified failure mode, helping teams prioritize corrective actions.

**Formula:**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Three Components:**
- **Severity (S):** A 1–10 rating of how seriously the failure would impact the customer.
- **Occurrence (O):** A 1–10 rating of how frequently the failure mode is likely to occur.
- **Detection (D):** A 1–10 rating of the difficulty of detecting the failure *before* it reaches the customer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Detection** with **Inspection**. Detection is the *probability of detection* — a high score = **hard to detect** (bad), a low score = **easy to detect** (good). This is a common 1-mark loss in KTU valuation.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Variant]**

**(a)** Define Benchmarking. Explain the **Robert C. Camp 5-Phase Benchmarking Process** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **applications of Benchmarking in the Service Sector and the IT Sector**, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Definition & 5-Phase Process (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Benchmarking is the continuous and systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against those of best-in-class performers — either within the same industry or across industries — to identify performance gaps and implement improvements.

**Robert C. Camp's 5-Phase Model (6 Marks):**

| **Phase** | **Key Activity** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Plan** | Identify the process to benchmark; define metrics; choose partner | Benchmarking plan, metric KPIs |
| **2. Analyze** | Collect As-Is data on own process; collect partner data; compute performance gap | Gap report |
| **3. Integrate** | Present findings; gain leadership acceptance; set improvement targets | Target setting & leadership buy-in |
| **4. Action** | Develop implementation plan; pilot changes; monitor | Process improvement & project plan |
| **5. Maturity** | Embed benchmarking as culture; refine practices; become best-in-class | Continuous improvement loop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point for KTU:** Phase 5 ensures that benchmarking is not a **one-time project** but a **continuous cycle** — emphasizing the "Maturity" phase makes the answer stand out.

**(b) Service Sector & IT Sector Applications (7 Marks):**

**Service Sector Applications (3.5 Marks):**
- **Hospitals:** Benchmarking average Emergency Room wait time against top hospitals (e.g., Apollo vs Max).
- **Airlines:** On-time performance, baggage handling, in-flight service comparison.
- **Banking:** Account opening time, loan processing turnaround, ATM uptime.
- **Hospitality:** Check-in time, room turnover time, guest satisfaction scores.

**IT Sector Applications (3.5 Marks):**
- **Help Desk / IT Support:** Average ticket resolution time, first-call resolution rate.
- **Software Development:** Defect density (defects per KLOC), sprint velocity, code review coverage.
- **Cloud Services:** SLA uptime (99.9% vs 99.99%), latency benchmarks.
- **IT Service Management:** Incident response time, MTTR, change success rate vs industry (using ITIL/COBIT benchmarks).
- **Example:** Infosys benchmarking its DevOps deployment frequency against global peers.

**Conclusion:** Benchmarking is a **sector-agnostic** improvement lever, but its metrics must be **tailored** to whether the process is physical (manufacturing), experiential (service), or digital (IT).

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Variant]**

**(a)** What is **FMEA**? Explain its **types** and list the **stages of conducting an FMEA**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** using the **6M framework**. Use a suitable example of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) FMEA – Types & Stages (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is a systematic, team-based, proactive technique used to identify potential failures in a product or process, evaluate their S/O/D, compute RPN, and prioritize corrective actions *before* failures reach the customer.

**Types of FMEA (3 Marks):**

| **Type** | **Scope** | **When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| **System FMEA** | Entire system / subsystem | Concept / architectural design phase |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Product design features | Detailed design phase |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing / assembly process | Process planning / production phase |

**Stages of FMEA (3 Marks):**

1. Assemble a **cross-functional team** (Design, Production, Quality, Service, Customer).
2. **Define scope** — boundaries, function, customer use.
3. **Identify failure modes** for each function (e.g., "Bearing seizes").
4. **Assess** Severity, Occurrence, Detection (each 1–10).
5. **Compute RPN** $= S \times O \times D$ and rank.
6. **Prioritize** failures (RPN > 200 first; 100–200 second).
7. **Recommend corrective actions** and assign owners.
8. **Reassess RPN** after actions and document lessons learned.

**(b) Fishbone Diagram — 6M Framework (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The Fishbone Diagram (developed by Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968) is a structured brainstorming tool that categorizes the potential root causes of a problem (the "effect") into major cause categories.

**Construction Steps (2 Marks):**

1. Define the **effect** clearly and place it on the right (fish head).
2. Draw the **main spine** (long arrow) pointing to the effect.
3. Draw **6 diagonal "bones"** for the 6M categories.
4. For each category, **brainstorm specific causes** as smaller sub-branches.
5. Apply the **5-Why technique** to drill down from each cause to the **root cause**.

**The 6M Categories (2 Marks):**

- **Man:** Operator skill, training, fatigue, motivation
- **Machine:** Equipment condition, calibration, capability
- **Material:** Quality, specifications, supplier, storage
- **Method:** Procedures, sequence, work instructions
- **Measurement:** Gauges, calibration, accuracy, sampling plan
- **Mother Nature:** Temperature, humidity, dust, light, ventilation

**Example (2 Marks):**
*Effect: Increase in defective products in an injection molding unit.*
- **Man:** New operator untrained on parameter setting
- **Machine:** Barrel heater band showing intermittent failure
- **Material:** Recycled resin mixed with virgin — ratio not controlled
- **Method:** SOP for temperature profile missing
- **Measurement:** No in-process temperature log; only final visual check
- **Mother Nature:** Monsoon humidity causing resin moisture absorption

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. In Q-A, students often **omit the RPN threshold table** (100/200) — losing 1 mark.
> 2. In Q-B (Fishbone), students write only the **names** of the 6Ms but **forget to give example causes** for each — losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. In Benchmarking, students confuse **"Functional"** with **"Competitive"** benchmarking. Recall: Competitive = *same industry*; Functional = *cross-industry*.
> 4. In DOE, students frequently **omit the random run-order** step — losing 1 mark in the procedure.
> 5. **Spelling trap:** "Ishikawa" is sometimes misspelled as "Ishkawa" / "Ishikawa" — usually ignored, but in MCQs it has caught students.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist the night before your KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Benchmarking** = continuous, systematic, comparative, and action-oriented. 4 Keywords.
- [ ] **Robert C. Camp 5-Phase Model:** Plan → Analyze → Integrate → Action → Maturity. *Mnemonic: **P-A-I-A-M**.*
- [ ] **Benchmarking Types:** Internal, Competitive, Functional, Strategic, Performance. *Memorize at least 3 with examples.*
- [ ] **FMEA RPN = S × O × D.** Threshold: **< 100** Accept, **100–200** Reduce, **> 200** Mandate action.
- [ ] **FMEA Types:** System, Design (DFMEA), Process (PFMEA). Each scored S/O/D 1–10.
- [ ] **Fishbone** = 6M (Manufacturing) / 4S (Service/IT). Use 5-Why after the diagram to find root cause.
- [ ] **Lean 5 Principles (Womack & Jones):** Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection. *Mnemonic: **V²-F-P-P**.*
- [ ] **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) + Skills:** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects.
- [ ] **Lean + TQM Integration:** TQM = philosophy (Why), Lean = tools (How). Combined = Lean TQM / LSS.
- [ ] **DOE Origin:** R. A. Fisher, 1920s, UK agriculture.
- [ ] **DOE Key Terms:** Factor, Level, Response, Replicate, Main Effect, Interaction Effect.
- [ ] **DOE Designs:** Full Factorial $2^k$ (all interactions), Fractional $2^{k-p}$ (screening), Taguchi OA (robust), RSM (optimization).
- [ ] **$2^2$ design formulas:** $\beta_0 = \Sigma y / 4$, main & interaction effects = (Σ signs × y) / 4.
- [ ] **Always randomize** DOE run order to avoid time-based bias.
- [ ] **Lean-TQM-Six Sigma Sigma-Combined = Lean Six Sigma (LSS)**, the modern industry standard.
- [ ] **Benchmarking ≠ Copying.** Adapt best practices, do not plagiarize.
- [ ] **In FMEA, Detection score:** *Lower = Better* (easy to detect); *Higher = Worse* (hard to detect).
- [ ] **Service benchmarking examples** to memorize: Hospital wait time, Airline OTD, IT Help-desk MTTR.
- [ ] **Fishbone = Ishikawa = Cause-and-Effect** — all three names are interchangeable in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark comparison question (TQM vs Lean, TQM vs Six Sigma, Benchmarking vs TQM), always end with a **small table** summarizing the comparison AND a concluding line on **integration/synergy**. Examiners explicitly award the last 1–2 marks for a structured conclusion.
